title: Data with Will
text:
繁體中文 | 简体中文
Greetings! My name is William Song, a data scientist and analyst based in Vancouver, BC, Canada.
My work sits at the intersection of business analytics, statistical modeling, data engineering, and visualization.
Like many of you, AI (LLMs in particular) has impacted my workflow in profound ways, and I am excited about the possibilities it opens up for data professionals. Also, I am aware of the potential risks it poses, technical-wise (security risk) and skill-wise (skill atrophy). That said, I also continue to write raw SQL and Python code from time to time to practice and to solve problems from the ground up.

title: Calculating Customer Lifetime Value
text:
The value of this project to me

Business analytics skills:

translating a core marketing/finance metric (CLV) into approachable formulas and examples for a non-technical audience.
comparing historical vs. predictive methods and understanding the trade-offs between simplicity, accuracy, and cost.

Communication:

structuring a technical topic into a 19-slide presentation with worked examples, suitable for stakeholders without a statistics background.


Presentation Slides



Overview
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV), also commonly known as LTV, estimates the total net profit a business can expect from a customer over the duration of their relationship. This presentation covers why CLV matters, the major families of CLV calculation methods, and worked examples for each.


Definition of a Cohort
Groups customers by acquisition period or channel (e.g., signup month or marketing channel) and tracks their cumulative revenue over time. The way of grouping can vary based on the business context and the insights sought.


Why CLV is hard
There is no single “correct” way to calculate CLV — every method involves assumptions and trade-offs.

Even well-built models typically carry 15–25% error margins.
CLV is usually applied to 3–7 year forecasting horizons, so small errors compound.
Every approach faces the same dilemma: a CLV model construction can be at most two of Simple, Accurate, and Inexpensive — never all three.




Historical CLV
Historical methods look backward at actual customer revenue/profit data.

ARPU / ARPA method
Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) or Average Revenue Per Account (ARPA) multiplies average revenue per customer by the expected customer lifespan.
Worked example — Alice vs. Bob
Alice: (150 + 50 + 100) / 6 months = $50 ARPU, 6 months lifespan → CLV = $300
Bob: (45 + 75 +100) / 2 months = $110 ARPU, 2 months lifespan → CLV = $220
(50 + 110) / 2 = $80 per customer
12 months value: $80 * 12 months = $960
24 months value: $80 * 24 months = $1,920

Alice spends less per month but stays subscribed much longer than Bob.
Even though Bob’s monthly spend is higher, Alice’s longer retention can make her CLV greater — illustrating why retention matters as much as spend.



Key points of Historical CLV methods
There are some factors that may not be captured by historical methods:

Different customers have different behavior patterns that may not be reflected in averages. Use cohort method to narrow down to more specific segments.
Future changes in customer behavior or market conditions may not be captured, such as internally changed by offerings or externally changed by market trends.
Not enough data when a business is too young or data duration is too short to capture the full customer lifecycle.





Predictive CLV
Predictive methods use statistical models to forecast future value rather than relying solely on historical averages.

Key Variables
Four variables drive most predictive CLV calculations:

Acquisition Cost / Retention Cost:

The cost to acquire a new customer or retain an existing one (e.g., marketing spend per conversion).

Net Profit Margin:

Among the cohorts, the share of revenue retained as profit after costs.
Profit Margin Ratio: Net Income / Net Sales
Usually the Finance team can provide this number, but if not, general rules are:

10% for public companies
15% for private companies that are scaling predictably
20%-25% for private companies that have not yet reached scale and predictable growth


Retention / Churn Rate and Expected Lifespan:

\[
\text{Retention Rate} = \frac{\text{Customers at End of Period} - \text{New Customers Acquired}}{\text{Customers at Start of Period}}
\]
The Churn Rate is the complement of the Retention Rate: \[
\text{Churn Rate} = 1 - \text{Retention Rate}
\]
We can use the retention rate to estimate the expected customer lifespan: \[
\text{Expected Customer Lifespan} = \frac{1}{\text{Churn Rate}}
\]

Discount Rate:

Accounts for the time value of money when projecting future profits to present value. Get this from the Finance team, or use 1 as a starting point for simplicity.



Formula
After getting the key variables, we can plug them into the formula to calculate CLV:

Simple formula
\[
CLV = \text{Average Revenue per Customer} \times \text{Expected Customer Lifespan}
\]


Complete formula
A more complete formula incorporates profit margins, retention, and the time value of money:
\[
CLV = \left( \frac{\text{Revenue per Customer} \times \text{Gross Margin}}{1 - \text{Retention Rate} + \text{Discount Rate}} \right) - \text{Acquisition Cost}
\]




Conclusion
CLV ultimately helps answer three fundamental business questions:

How much should we spend to acquire a customer?
Which customer segments are worth investing in retaining?
How sustainable is our current growth strategy?

Choosing a CLV method is less about finding the “perfect” formula and more about matching the right balance of simplicity, accuracy, and cost to the decision at hand.



References

For Entrepreneurs — What’s your TRUE customer lifetime value (LTV)?
Kaushik.net — Excellent Analytics Tip #17: Calculate Customer Lifetime Value
Above the Crowd — The Dangerous Seduction of the Lifetime Value (LTV) Formula
Wikipedia — Customer Lifetime Value

title: World Education Project
text:
The value of this group project to me

Technical skills:

deploying a Shiny dashboard to share insights interactively on Posit Cloud, a great substitute if Tableau or Power BI are not available.
applying data cleaning, EDA, visualization, and dashboarding techniques.

Collaboration:

working with a team of 4 data science students to divide tasks, share insights, and integrate work into a cohesive project.

Project management:

coordinating timelines, version control, and communication to ensure smooth progress.





Overview
This project analyzes global education indicators to explore patterns in enrollment, attainment, and inequality across countries. The analysis was completed as part of UBC Master of Data Science, DSCI 532 Data Visualization course and demonstrates data cleaning, exploratory analysis, visualization, and basic modeling to surface insights relevant to education gaps between genders and international regions. Then finally, deploy the insights in an interactive dashboard built with Shiny, and a chatbot interface using a large language model to query the data.


Project repository
Forked project repo: World Education Project



Data
Primary files (from the repo)

data/processed/processed_global_education.csv: cleaned, merged dataset used for analysis.

data/raw/Global_Education.csv: raw source files sourced from Kaggle - World Educational Data, compiled from UNESCO Institute for Statistics, UNICEF, and UN Statistics Division.
notebooks/EDA.ipynb: analysis jupyter notebooks (Python) used to produce figures and tables.




Methods & Analytics

Data cleaning & integration: harmonized country codes, handled missingness, merged multiple sources.

Exploratory data analysis: summary statistics, distributions, and correlation matrices.
Segmentation: country clustering by region, gender, education outcomes and socioeconomic indicators.
Visualization: maps, comparative bar charts, correlation scatter plot.
Chatbot using LLM: used a large language model to automatically filter and explore the data.




Dashboard
Link to the dashboard
We built an interactive dashboard using Shiny app to allow users to explore the data and insights dynamically. The dashboard includes: World map views with metric selector, bar charts comparing average education level by region, and completion rate gap by region, scatter plot comparing male and female literacy rate by region, and a chatbot interface for querying the data using natural language.

Landing page 
Interactive plots 
Chatbot page

title: Species name search
text: This project builds a production-grade species name search tool that aggregates results from over 15 external biodiversity databases including GBIF and Symbiota portals.

title: Species name search
text: This project builds a production-grade species name search tool that aggregates results from over 15 external biodiversity databases including GBIF and Symbiota portals.

title: Species name search
text:
Problem
Biodiversity researchers often need to reconcile species names across multiple taxonomic authorities. No single API covers all databases, and sequential requests are too slow for interactive use.

title: Species name search
text:
Approach

Async fan-out: Used httpx with asyncio.gather to query all APIs concurrently, reducing median latency from ~8s sequential to ~1.2s.
Redis caching: Common species names are cached with a 24-hour TTL, cutting repeat query cost to near zero.
PostgreSQL: Stores canonical name mappings and user search history.
Next.js frontend: Displays ranked results with source attribution and fuzzy match scores.

title: Species name search
text:
Key code
import asyncio
import httpx

async def fan_out(name: str, sources: list[str]) -> dict:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0) as client:
        tasks = [query_source(client, name, src) for src in sources]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    return {src: r for src, r in zip(sources, results) if not isinstance(r, Exception)}

title: Species name search
text:
Results

Sub-2s response time across 15 sources
94% cache hit rate on common queries after first week
Deployed on a single $6/month VPS with no downtime in 3 months

Python handles what you do with it
Together they cover the full pipeline: extract → transform → analyze → visualize

Data Wrangling with pandas
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")

# Filter, group, aggregate
result = (
    df[df["year"] == 2024]
    .groupby("category")["revenue"]

title: Data Science with Python & SQL
text:
Query Optimization Basics

Use indexes on columns you filter or join on
Avoid SELECT * — only fetch what you need
Push filters early, before joins
Use EXPLAIN to inspect query plans

-- Before
SELECT * FROM orders JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.id;

-- After
SELECT orders.id, customers.name
FROM orders
JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.id
WHERE orders.status = 'completed';

title: Data Science with Python & SQL
text:
Visualization Tips

Match chart type to your question

Distribution → histogram or boxplot
Trend → line chart
Comparison → bar chart

Label axes clearly
Avoid chartjunk

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text: Both DuckDB and pandas can handle local analytical workloads, but they have very different performance profiles. After hitting pandas memory limits on a 5M-row CSV, I did a side-by-side comparison.

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text: Both DuckDB and pandas can handle local analytical workloads, but they have very different performance profiles. After hitting pandas memory limits on a 5M-row CSV, I did a side-by-side comparison.

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text:
The short answer
Use DuckDB when:

Your dataset is larger than ~500MB
You are doing aggregations, joins, or window functions
You want to query Parquet or CSV files without loading them into memory

Use pandas when:

You need row-wise Python logic (.apply, custom functions)
You are doing iterative data cleaning with lots of intermediate steps
The dataset fits comfortably in memory and transformation is simple

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text:
Benchmark
import duckdb
import pandas as pd
import time

# DuckDB aggregation
start = time.perf_counter()
duckdb.sql("SELECT category, AVG(value) FROM 'data.parquet' GROUP BY category").df()
print(f"DuckDB: {time.perf_counter() - start:.2f}s")

# pandas equivalent
start = time.perf_counter()
df = pd.read_parquet("data.parquet")
df.groupby("category")["value"].mean()
print(f"pandas: {time.perf_counter() - start:.2f}s")
On 5M rows: DuckDB finished in 0.3s, pandas in 2.1s – and pandas used 1.4GB RAM vs DuckDB’s 180MB.

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text:
Takeaway
DuckDB is not a replacement for pandas. They compose well: use DuckDB to filter and aggregate down to a manageable size, then hand off to pandas for Python-native transformations.

title: Module 4: Query Optimization
text: Practical techniques for writing faster, more efficient SQL queries.

title: Module 4: Query Optimization
text:
Use Indexes

Add indexes on columns you frequently filter or join on
Avoid full table scans on large datasets

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);

title: Module 4: Query Optimization
text:
Push Filters Early
-- Slow: joins first, then filters
SELECT * FROM orders
JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.id
WHERE orders.status = 'completed';

-- Fast: filter before join
SELECT o.id, c.name
FROM (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed') o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id;

title: SQL query optimization study
text: A structured benchmarking study comparing common SQL patterns – CTEs vs subqueries, window functions vs GROUP BY aggregations – on large datasets in both PostgreSQL 16 and BigQuery.

title: SQL query optimization study
text: A structured benchmarking study comparing common SQL patterns – CTEs vs subqueries, window functions vs GROUP BY aggregations – on large datasets in both PostgreSQL 16 and BigQuery.

title: SQL query optimization study
text:
Motivation
Much SQL advice online is untested or dialect-specific. I wanted concrete, reproducible numbers for the patterns I use most often.

title: SQL query optimization study
text:
Method

Generated synthetic datasets of 10M rows using Python’s faker library
Ran each query 10 times and recorded median wall time
Controlled for caching by running DISCARD ALL between trials in Postgres

title: SQL query optimization study
text:
Selected findings



Pattern
PostgreSQL
BigQuery




CTE (non-recursive)
1.2s
0.8s


Equivalent subquery
1.1s
0.9s


Window ROW_NUMBER()
0.7s
0.4s


GROUP BY + join
1.4s
0.6s



CTEs in PostgreSQL are not optimization fences as of v12+, which surprised many colleagues who avoided them for performance reasons.

title: SQL query optimization study
text:
Code
SELECT
  user_id,
  event_date,
  SUM(revenue) OVER (
    PARTITION BY user_id
    ORDER BY event_date
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS cumulative_revenue
FROM events;

title: Model Interpretation
text:
The value of this group project to me

Technical skills:

creating reusable functions and scripts for data preprocessing, modeling, and evaluation to promote reproducibility and scalability.
exploring new platform Netlify for hosting and sharing data science projects.
applying data cleaning, EDA, machine learning models (baseline, KNN, SVM RBF), and visualization techniques.

Collaboration:

working with a team of 3 data science students to divide tasks, share insights, and integrate work into a cohesive project.

Project management:

coordinating timelines, version control, and communication to ensure smooth progress.
pushing code to GitHub and deploying the final report on Netlify for public access.





Overview
model_auto_interpret is a Python package that streamlines the repetitive parts of building and comparing scikit-learn classification models — hyperparameter tuning, cross-model comparison, and performance evaluation. Rather than rewriting the same boilerplate for every project, the package wraps these steps into three reusable functions, sitting in the gap between scikit-learn’s low-level utilities and visualization-only libraries like mlxtend. The package was built as part of UBC Master of Data Science, DSCI 524 Collaborative Software Development course, with a team of 3: Daisy (Ying) Zhou, Yasaman Baher, and myself.


Project repository
GitHub repo: UBC-MDS/model_interpretation (forked from school repo because it is not publicly accessible)


Documentation & tutorial
Docs site: machine-learning-model-interpretation.netlify.app — deployed on Netlify, including a full tutorial and API reference.



Installation
pip install -i https://test.pypi.org/simple/ model-auto-interpret



Key Functions
The package centers on three functions covering the typical model-development workflow:

param_tuning_summary: takes a fitted GridSearchCV/RandomizedSearchCV object and returns a clean summary dataframe of the best parameters and scores, plus the best estimator ready to use.
model_cv_metric_compare: evaluates multiple unfitted models side-by-side via cross-validation, returning accuracy, precision, recall, F1, and ROC-AUC in a single comparison table.
model_evaluation_plotting: scores a fitted model on held-out test data, returning accuracy/F2 metrics, a confusion matrix table, and a ready-to-plot confusion matrix display.




Tutorial walkthrough
The package tutorial demonstrates the full workflow on the Breast Cancer dataset (455 training samples, 30 features, binary classification):

Compare models: model_cv_metric_compare() runs 5-fold cross-validation across Logistic Regression, SVC variants, Random Forest, Decision Tree, and Gradient Boosting, producing a single comparison table.
Tune hyperparameters: param_tuning_summary() extracts the best parameter configuration from GridSearchCV/RandomizedSearchCV results for the top candidate models.
Evaluate on test data: model_evaluation_plotting() scores the tuned model on the test set and generates a confusion matrix visualization.

Result: Random Forest emerged as the top performer at ~96% cross-validation accuracy. The tutorial closes with an end-to-end example on the Iris dataset, reaching 100% test accuracy.



Key takeaways

Less boilerplate, more reproducibility: collapsing tuning, comparison, and evaluation into three function calls makes model-development notebooks shorter and easier for teammates to rerun.
Designed for collaboration: built with packaging best practices (tests, CI, semantic versioning, hosted docs) as the core learning objective of the course, not just the modeling logic itself.

title: Smart Amazon Product Query Assistant
text:
The value of this project to me

Technical skills:

implementing and comparing three retrieval approaches (BM25, semantic search, hybrid) on a large real-world product dataset.
building a full RAG pipeline, from retrieval through context construction to LLM generation, with a Streamlit chat interface.
working with vector search (FAISS) and sentence embeddings, plus comparing two different LLMs for the generation step.

Collaboration:

working with a teammate across two milestones, dividing work between retrieval methods and the generation/app layer.

Project management:

structuring the project as incremental milestones, each with its own discussion and evaluation writeup, and documenting setup so the app can be reproduced from a fresh environment.





Overview
This project is a context-aware product search assistant for Amazon listings. Given a natural language query, it retrieves relevant products and can generate a conversational answer grounded in product metadata and reviews. It was built for UBC Master of Data Science, DSCI 575 Advanced Machine Learning course, with teammate Harrison Li.


Project repository
GitHub repo: DSCI_575_project_hli76_wnsong (forked from school repo because it is not publicly accessible)



Dataset

Source: Amazon Reviews 2023 dataset (McAuley-Lab, via Hugging Face), Appliances category.
Scale: 94,319 products, 2,128,605 total rows of product metadata (titles, descriptions, features, pricing) and aggregated user reviews.




Architecture
The project progressed through two milestones, layering generation on top of retrieval.

Milestone 1: Retrieval methods

BM25: traditional keyword matching, with scores normalized via max scaling.
Semantic search: dense embeddings from all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (sentence-transformers), indexed in FAISS using IndexFlatIP for L2-normalized inner product (cosine similarity) search.
Hybrid retrieval: combines both methods by normalizing each score to [0, 1] and blending them, hybrid_score = alpha * norm_sem + (1 - alpha) * norm_bm25, with alpha defaulting to 0.5.



Milestone 2: RAG pipeline

Workflow: user query, retriever selection, document retrieval, context building, LLM generation, response shown in the chat UI.
Primary model: Llama-3.3-70b-versatile via the Groq API, chosen for strong instruction-following and summarization, and for its large context window to handle the “lost in the middle” problem with lengthy product reviews. Groq’s LPU hardware also gives fast inference.
Alternative model: gpt-oss-20b, included for side-by-side comparison against the primary model.





App
The assistant runs as a Streamlit chat app (app/app.py). Users pick a retrieval method and LLM, ask a question in natural language, and get back a grounded answer along with the retrieved products. LLM features require a .env file with GROQ_API_KEY and TAVILY_API_KEY (the latter for web search augmentation). A demo video app-demo.mp4 walks through the app in the repo.



Installation & usage
git clone https://github.com/canadasung/DSCI_575_project_hli76_wnsong.git
cd DSCI_575_project_hli76_wnsong
conda env create -f environment.yml
conda activate <env-name>
python src/download_data.py
streamlit run app/app.py
The data pipeline produces raw parquet files, processed documents and product indices, a BM25 pickle index with tokenized corpus, and a FAISS vector index for semantic search. User feedback collected from the app is logged to CSV.



Results & evaluation
Detailed findings and evaluation discussion for each milestone are documented in the repo under results/milestone1_discussion.md and results/milestone2_discussion.md.



Key takeaways

Retrieval choice matters: BM25, semantic search, and hybrid retrieval each have different strengths, and blending them gave more flexibility than committing to one method.
Model size versus speed tradeoff: comparing a 70B and a 20B model surfaced practical tradeoffs between answer quality and inference cost/speed, which Groq’s hardware helped offset.

title: Washrooms in Vancouver Parks
text:
The value of this group project to me

Technical skills:

creating a containerized environment using Docker to practice a reproducible workflow.
applying data cleaning, EDA, machine learning models (baseline, KNN, SVM RBF), and visualization techniques.

Collaboration:

working with a team of 3 data science students to divide tasks, share insights, and integrate work into a cohesive project.

Project management:

coordinating timelines, version control, and communication to ensure smooth progress.





Overview
This project maps and analyzes public washroom availability in Vancouver parks. It combines spatial data, park metadata, and accessibility attributes to identify coverage gaps and inform planning decisions for park amenities. The analysis was completed as part of UBC Master of Data Science, DSCI 522 Data Science Workflow course.


Project repository
Forked project repo: washrooms-in-vancouver-parks



Report
The final report can be found here: Predicting the Presence of Washrooms in Vancouver Parks.



Data

Source and scope

City of Vancouver Parks dataset (Vancouver 2025) covering 218 parks with facility attributes, locations, and sizes.



Key variables used

Target: washrooms (presence/absence).
Predictors: park size (hectares), neighbourhood, official park flag, advisories, counts of additional facilities, and special features.



Notes and limitations

The dataset is cross‑sectional and may omit contextual features (visitor counts, funding cycles) that could improve predictive performance.





Methods & Analytics

Exploratory analysis

Compare distributions of binary amenities and park size between parks with and without washrooms (frequency plots, histograms).



Modeling approach

Baseline: Dummy classifier to set a reference accuracy.
Models tested: K‑Nearest Neighbors (KNN) and Support Vector Machine with RBF kernel (SVM RBF).
Validation: 70/30 train/test split with 5‑fold cross‑validation on training data.
Preprocessing: Standardize numeric features; encode categorical predictors (neighbourhood, flags); handle missingness prior to fitting.



Evaluation metrics

Cross‑validation test score, training score, and confusion matrix on held‑out test set to assess generalization and class balance.





Key Findings

Parks with washrooms tend to be larger: median hectare is higher for parks that include washrooms.
Washroom presence correlates with other amenities: parks offering more facilities are more likely to have washrooms.
SVM RBF outperformed KNN and the baseline: in cross‑validation, though the improvement over the dummy baseline was modest, indicating limited predictive signal in the chosen features.
KNN showed signs of overfitting: (higher training score, lower test performance) and therefore generalized poorly.
Implication: richer features (e.g., visitor counts, park usage, funding/prioritization data) or alternative modeling strategies are likely needed to produce stronger, actionable predictions.




Visualizations

Model comparison: 
Confusion matrix:

title: Market Basket Analysis
text:
The value of this project to me

Business analytics skills:

applying affinity analysis (Market Basket Analysis) to uncover product co-occurrence patterns from transactional data.
translating data mining concepts (Support, Confidence, Lift) into a practical, business-friendly cross-selling tool.

Communication:

presenting a technical analytics method to business stakeholders using a worked example (bread & butter) and clear SMART criteria.


Presentation Slides



Overview
Market Basket Analysis (MBA) — also known as affinity analysis — is a data mining technique based on conditional probability (Bayes’ Theorem) used to discover co-occurrence relationships among products (the classic “beers and diapers” example). It is widely adopted across retail, e-commerce, and finance for cross-sell/upsell strategy, bundling and discounting, shelf or page placement, and fraud detection.



Why Market Basket Analysis

Easy to understand and implement: gather transactional data with a defined “basket” (e.g., by customer or by transaction).
Widely applicable: cross-sell/upsell, bundle/discount strategy, shelf or webpage display optimization, and fraud pattern detection in finance.




Analytic Terms

Occurrence & Support
Occurrence / Co-occurrence: the number of times an item (or set of items) is purchased within the group.
Support: the frequency (ratio) of an occurrence within the group.
Worked example — out of 100 customers:

Bread is purchased by 50 customers → Support(Bread) = 50/100 = 0.5
Butter is purchased by 30 customers → Support(Butter) = 30/100 = 0.3
Bread and Butter together are purchased 20 times → Support(Co-occurrence) = 20/100 = 0.2



Confidence
Confidence: the probability of both items being purchased, given that one item was purchased.
\[
Confidence(Bread) = \frac{Support(Co\text{-}occur)}{Support(Bread)} = \frac{0.2}{0.5} = 0.4
\]
\[
Confidence(Butter) = \frac{Support(Co\text{-}occur)}{Support(Butter)} = \frac{0.2}{0.3} = 0.6667
\]


Lift
Lift: the key indicator — the likelihood that two products go together. Only meaningful when greater than 1 (the higher, the stronger the association), though there is no universal market standard for “high enough”; values are interpreted relative to other outputs.
\[
Lift(Bread \,\&\, Butter) = \frac{Support(Co\text{-}occur)}{Support(Bread) \times Support(Butter)} = \frac{0.2}{0.15} = 1.33
\]




Benefits of MBA

Accurate and easy to read: Support, Confidence, and Lift are simple, interpretable metrics.
Automation is possible: all products analyzed in under 10 minutes, versus manual correlation analysis comparing only 3–4 products per day.
Balances data cleanliness vs. time cost: different levels of data cleanliness still reveal similar patterns.
Additional insights: product popularity, and comparisons across years, seasons, or stores.




Constraints

Similar product formats are still compared: e.g., 1L milk vs. 2L milk. Mitigate by using filtering features to screen products.
Basic MBA is limited to 1-to-1 relationships: e.g., product A and product B. Advanced MBA (A + B + C → D) requires more advanced software/services.
Data cleaning and model maintenance:

Data training is required.
Raw data needs cleaning to reduce model run time.
Procedures must be modified or re-created if the analysis goals change (e.g., changes to the basket or product hierarchy).





Conclusion — SMART in Creating MBA Analytics

Specific: can be measured under different product hierarchies.
Measurable: Support, Confidence, and Lift indicators support decision-making.
Achievable: cost-effective when there’s no budget for external software licenses for the functionality and require self-build.
Relevant: directly matches cross-selling strategy goals.
Timeliness: automation enables fast, easy-to-learn results.
